text: Maps compress the world into something holdable. They strip away noise and render only what matters: roads, rivers, borders, elevation. A good map is a decision about what to include and what to leave out, which makes it as much an argument as a representation. Before GPS, reading a map was a skill — you had to orient yourself, find landmarks, reason through distance. Now most people just follow the blue dot. That works fine until it doesn't. Maps also lie in interesting ways. Every flat projection distorts a sphere. Greenland looks enormous. Antarctica disappears or bloats. The map is not the territory, as the saying goes, but it shapes how we think about the territory all the same.
